storyboardTitle: May 12, 2022 Derecho — Live Severe Weather Simulator v5

cards.title: SPC Outlook
narrative: It is 3:00 PM CDT. Start with the SPC outlook and explain why the setup was already concerning before the biggest impacts hit.
A key teaching question here is how many people really knew that a high-end wind event was possible at this point in the day.

cards.title: PDS Watch
narrative: The Particularly Dangerous Situation watch is a pivotal product for this event.
Use this card to stress the possibility of 100+ mph winds and why that should have raised the operational level of concern.

cards.title: Future Radar
narrative: Future radar helps frame where the line may be heading before the event reaches the peak impact period.
This is a forecast product, so it is one of the few cards that can legitimately look ahead of the master clock.

cards.title: Future Winds
narrative: Use future wind guidance to discuss the potential for damaging straight-line winds ahead of time.
This is a good place to compare forecast confidence versus what the public might actually perceive.

cards.title: Satellite
narrative: Satellite is part of the preparation phase from 3 to 4 PM.
Talk through cloud trends, clearing, and how meteorologists read environmental clues before the warnings dominate the screen.

cards.title: Radar Baseline
narrative: Use a quick radar baseline so students understand where storms are before the event ramps up.
This is not yet the peak severe phase, but it sets up everything that follows.

cards.title: Radar Focus
narrative: By 4 PM, radar becomes one of the main teaching tools.
This is where the simulator should start feeling more urgent as storms organize and approach the region.

cards.title: Live Doppler
narrative: Live Doppler is one of the signature meteorology moments in this simulator.
Use it to explain why wind information matters and how Doppler reveals the strength of the event beyond simple reflectivity.

cards.title: Warnings
narrative: The first batch of warnings is starting to matter more now.
This is where the operational side of the simulator begins to feel live and consequential.

cards.title: Radar + Warnings
narrative: Radar plus warnings is a strong combination card for students because it ties what they see to official action.
Use this when you want the map to feel like a TV cut-in.

cards.title: First Reports
narrative: Early reports begin adding reality to the radar picture.
One major moment to build in later is the 107 mph report that captured the seriousness of the wind threat.

cards.title: Lightning Trends
narrative: Lightning may or may not be central to every moment, but trends can still be discussed.
Eventually, the lightning layer should flash strikes from the last 10 to 15 minutes on and off to emphasize recent activity.

cards.title: Radar at Peak
narrative: Radar is huge at 5 PM and should probably remain the default anchor product.
This is where the line evolution becomes the story.

cards.title: Radar + Reports
narrative: Radar plus storm reports is ideal for showing how impacts confirm what the radar is suggesting.
This is a key combination for teaching real-time verification.

cards.title: Radar + Lightning + Warnings
narrative: This combination card is intentionally busier and should be used when you want the screen to feel operationally intense.
Students can compare the warning footprint to where the active lightning and strongest radar returns are appearing.

cards.title: Live Doppler + Warnings
narrative: This is one of the best cards for showing the power of Doppler in a damaging-wind event.
Use this to explain that velocity data often tells a more direct wind story than reflectivity alone.

cards.title: Storm Photos
narrative: Photos begin to matter more as the event matures and the human side of the storm becomes visible.
This will be a strong future card once the damage and storm images are tied to the timeline.

cards.title: Radar Continuation
narrative: The event is still ongoing and radar remains critical.
This is where the story shifts from isolated moments to the broader sweep of a major windstorm.

cards.title: Warnings in Effect
narrative: Use this card to talk about warning count, overlap, and how busy a warning operation can become in a major event.
The warnings-in-effect box should reinforce that live operational feel.

cards.title: Widening Damage Reports
narrative: Storm reports help the class see that the story is no longer limited to one county or one warning polygon.
The severe wind damage is becoming regional.

cards.title: Damage Photos
narrative: Use this card once photos and media are available for the 6 PM phase.
This is a strong bridge between the map and the human impact.

cards.title: Storms Shift North
narrative: By 7 PM the event is not over, but for this simulator it is probably enough to wind down the core one-hour lesson.
Storms are shifting north of Sioux Falls, so this becomes a good transition toward recap.

cards.title: Report Summary
narrative: Use the 7 PM reports card to summarize what the class learned from impacts versus warnings and radar evolution.
This is a strong place to ask what was most important operationally.
